translation: By the night when it covers
commentary:
وَٱلَّيْلِ إِذَا يَغْشَىٰ

I swear by the night as it covers. The surah begins with divine oaths, continuing the pattern from previous Makkan surahs. The night is mentioned as it spreads and covers everything in darkness, concealing what was visible during the day.

translation: And [by] the day when it appears
commentary:
وَٱلنَّهَارِ إِذَا تَجَلَّىٰ

And by the day as it reveals itself in full glory. The word تَجَلَّىٰ comes from the same root as a bald man's shiny head—completely exposed and brilliant. When the daytime arrives, everything becomes clear, obvious, and visible. This establishes the fundamental contrast: night covers and conceals, while day exposes and illuminates.

translation: And [by] He who created the male and female
commentary:
وَمَا خَلَقَ ٱلذَّكَرَ وَٱلْأُنثَىٰ

And by how remarkably He created the male and the female. This third and final oath is more concise than the series of oaths in previous surahs. While the previous surah (Ash-Shams) emphasized the spiritual balance within each human being, this surah highlights the contrast outside ourselves—how Allah ﷻ physically created us as two very different creatures: men and women.

Throughout the Quran, the relationship between men and women is compared to the relationship between night and day. They are extreme opposites yet need one another, and without each other, life cannot continue. Both have dimensions of each other—night slowly transitions into day, and day gradually turns into night. There is a natural diffusion and mixture between the two, similar to the relationship between men and women.

The night is associated with rest, tranquility, peace, and calm. It is the time when everything becomes still—animals sleep, plants are quiet, the world stops moving. This mirrors one dimension of the relationship between husband and wife, where they are supposed to find peace, tranquility, and love in each other, especially during the night when the world is away and they are alone together: لِتَسْكُنُوا إِلَيْهَا (that you may find tranquility in them).

The day, however, brings activity, movement, commerce, and responsibility. Most responsibilities are fulfilled during daylight. This represents the other side of the relationship between men and women—working together to fulfill responsibilities, taking care of business, ensuring things are accomplished. A healthy relationship requires both dimensions: love, tranquility, and romance on one side; responsibility, diligence, and partnership on the other. You cannot have one without the other.

The Arabic words themselves reveal profound meanings. The root of ذَكَر (male) relates to hardness, toughness, rigidity, and strength. A sword is called mudakkar because it is hard and unbending. The metal top of an ax is also associated with this root. Intense rain that is hard on the body is called dhakar. Men were created by Allah ﷻ to be tough—physically and in personality. Generally, men are far stronger physically than women, and typically possess less emotional intelligence.

The word أُنثَىٰ (female), on the other hand, relates to softness, flexibility, and the capacity to nurture life. It describes a patch of land that is easy to work with, soft, and ripe for growth. By nature, according to Arab linguistic tradition, a woman is meant to be soft, while a man is meant to be tougher. This doesn't mean women cannot be tough or men cannot be soft, but rather that for a relationship to work—like night slowly transitioning to day—both must adapt. Sometimes softness must become tougher, and sometimes toughness must become gentler. They must work together despite their fundamental differences.

translation: Indeed, your efforts are diverse
commentary:
إِنَّ سَعْيَكُمْ لَشَتَّىٰ

Without doubt, your efforts are truly diverse. After these oaths of contrast and complementary nature, after describing the struggle between night and day and the differences between men and women, Allah ﷻ arrives at the central statement of the surah. Everything else in the surah essentially explains this ayah.

The word شَتَّىٰ means scattered, distributed, spread out in many different directions. Allah ﷻ is declaring that the efforts you make in this life—the things you work toward, the things you strive for—are going in completely different directions, as different as night and day, as contrasting as male and female, as divergent as soft and hard. Humanity is making efforts in every possible direction.

Some understand this to mean that what you are earning is completely different from one another. Your pursuits, your goals, your ambitions—they all vary drastically. The question then becomes: What are these contrasting efforts heading in such opposite directions? The following ayahs describe two paths.

## Ayahs 5-7

فَأَمَّا مَنْ أَعْطَىٰ وَٱتَّقَىٰ
وَصَدَّقَ بِٱلْحُسْنَىٰ
فَسَنُيَسِّرُهُۥ لِلْيُسْرَىٰ

As for the one who gave, and was conscious of Allah, and accepted the ultimate truth, then We will facilitate for him the path to ultimate ease.

These three verbs appear in a progressive order, presenting a beautiful and profound sequence that captures the moral worldview of the Quran.

**أَعْطَىٰ - Gave**: The root means to extend by hand, to give whatever you can reach to somebody else. This represents giving as much as you can. Allah ﷻ doesn't specify what is given—He simply says the person is giving. When you have a chance to give someone time, a smile, charity, help, or advice—whatever you can give—you are a giving person. This word encapsulates our goodness toward humanity. A parent giving their child love, a teacher giving students patience, a generous person giving charity to the poor, an expert giving advice, someone volunteering their time—all forms of giving. Allah ﷻ says: أَنفِقُوا مِمَّا رَزَقْنَاكُمْ (Spend out of what We have provided you). Some have been given talent, some strength, some money, some wisdom. Whatever Allah ﷻ has given you, you give it back. You don't hold it within yourself—you keep giving.

**وَٱتَّقَىٰ - Was conscious and fearful**: Taqwa means to protect yourself, to be cautious and careful. While the first quality addressed our relationship with humanity, this second quality addresses our relationship with Allah ﷻ. The first dimension is being good to people; the second is being good to Allah ﷻ. These two words together capture a comprehensive picture: goodness toward all creation and consciousness of the Creator.

There are people in the world who are humanitarian—generous, kind, good coworkers, excellent friends—whether Christian, Jewish, Hindu, or of other faiths. They possess the quality of giving, but they are missing consciousness of Allah ﷻ. One level of goodness without the second.

**وَصَدَّقَ بِٱلْحُسْنَىٰ - And accepted the ultimate truth**: The word الْحُسْنَىٰ means "the most beautiful," so this phrase translates as "accepted the truth in the most beautiful." Some interpret al-husna as the most beautiful word (meaning لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا ٱللَّٰهُ), while others say it refers to the most beautiful deeds or good actions.

To understand this profound concept, we must first examine the word صَدَّقَ. In Arabic, even abstract ideas like truth have concrete origins. The root of صِدْق (truth) means something solid, something that doesn't budge. The opposite—lying—is actually something soft, constantly adjusting to circumstances. Truth is unchanging and uncompromising. The word sidq carries within it the meaning of "hard truth." When you say saddaqa, you are accepting something whether it's comfortable or not, easy or not. Truth is truth, finished.

Now consider what this means in everyday experience. You could be charitable and spiritual in your worship, but there's a problem: you could be wrong. There are times when you hold an incorrect opinion or practice something mistaken. When someone discusses Islam with a practicing Christian who is charitable and spiritual, and during discussion that person gets cornered and realizes what you're saying is true—at that moment, hesitation kicks in. "I know you're making sense, but I'm not comfortable accepting this religion. Have you heard what people say about Muslims in the media? I would be associated with that. Even though what you're saying makes sense, it's not an easy call for me." Or pride kicks in: "No way am I going to accept that I'm wrong."

When you hold firmly to a view and someone comes along saying, "There's actually a better truth. There's something you need to know. You need to correct yourself"—imagine my own example: I give a lecture and share something about an ayah or surah, and someone approaches saying, "What you shared was actually incorrect. You made mistakes here, here, and here." Immediately in my head, I realize what they're saying makes sense. But people are watching. What will he say now? I could respond, "Well, there are other ways of looking at it. There's a difference of opinion." Or I could simply say, "You know what? You're right. I was wrong. I'm going to fix that."

At that moment, if something is presented to me—despite the hesitation, despite the social pressure of "he got corrected"—and I simply accept that the more beautiful truth has been presented and I accept it without hesitation, without fear of consequence, because truth is truth and I will humble myself before it, this is the ultimate goodness a person can reach. This is its height.

This completes the first two qualities. Perhaps you're wrong toward someone, and somebody approaches you saying, "You're not being right toward this person. You should have done it this way." Your first inclination, your ego's response, is "Yeah, I understand, but I have my reasons." You don't want to accept that you've done something incorrect. You want to justify, to rationalize.

Similarly, when it comes to religion itself—someone saying, "Islamically, what you're doing is wrong" or "What you believe contradicts this. Look at the example of the Prophet ﷺ. Look at this truth"—at that moment you respond, "You're not a scholar. I believe what I believe." It becomes hard to accept the truth when it's presented to you immediately. But when the beauty and truth of something—when the most beautiful truth—is presented to you, and you hesitate in no way to accept it, that is the station of someone who, when truth comes, accepts it immediately regardless of consequences. That's it. That's what I'm going to take. Good enough for me.

This is why they say this surah is about Abu Bakr As-Siddiq (RA). When the truth of the Prophet ﷺ was presented to him, there was no hesitation, no time for processing. "Yes, you are the messenger. I testify." There was no "Let me think about it for a couple of days. Let me consider the implications. What will my family think? What will society think? What are people saying about you? This is going to be difficult. We're going to lose everything. They'll kick us out. Nobody will do business with us anymore." No thought of consequence—he just accepted it. This is the height of tasdiq bil-husna.

The beautiful insight here is that the natural development of a human being begins in childhood with experiencing the love of parents. Our first experience of goodness is actually goodness toward humanity—family, then friends, then society. If a person has that natural goodness in them, they will actually be inclined toward goodness to Allah ﷻ. It will naturally follow. And when that taqwa comes, these two things together will be perfected by tasdiq bil-husna.

This is the worldview of the Quran. Today we often think of Islam as a set of principles, rules, and guidelines. We don't think of Islam first and foremost as a worldview that necessitates how we are toward people, then toward Allah ﷻ as a result, and then entirely submissive to truth. May Allah ﷻ make us from among them.

**فَسَنُيَسِّرُهُۥ لِلْيُسْرَىٰ - We will facilitate for him the path to ultimate ease**: This is a complex and beautiful statement with multiple layers of meaning.

The word يُيَسِّرُ in Arabic means to help something grow little by little and strengthen it. For example, taysir of a camel is when it becomes healthy enough to give more and more milk—reaching its potential. When you're working out and a coach helps you lift more and more weight, he's doing taysir—empowering and enabling you to make harder things easier. From this root comes the word يُسْر (ease), but the concept is that your path is being facilitated as you are being strengthened.

Allah ﷻ is saying: For a person who accomplishes those three things—giving, being conscious of Allah ﷻ, and accepting the most beautiful truth whenever it comes to them—We will facilitate their path, strengthen them, empower them, and enable them all the way to the ultimate ease. What is the ultimate ease? It is Paradise, when the struggle of this life is over and we finally get to relax before Allah ﷻ. The theme of previous surahs was struggle, toil, and labor—the fate of humanity. Now Allah ﷻ says He will take you all the way to where there is ease, no struggle, no labor, no toil. That is لِلْيُسْرَىٰ.

One way to understand this sequence: if you practice these behaviors—giving, taqwa, accepting truth—Allah ﷻ will give you the strength to make it all the way to Paradise.

Another profound way to look at these ayahs: As you are doing those things, this is how Allah ﷻ enables you. This is Allah ﷻ facilitating your path. When you say "May Allah ﷻ make my road easy" or "May Allah ﷻ give me guidance," Allah ﷻ is essentially saying in this ayah: If you are in fact a giving person, that's Me making the road easy for you. If you're developing taqwa—becoming more fearful, mindful, and remorseful over mistakes—that's Me facilitating it for you. If you're accepting truth the moment you encounter it, this is My divine gift to you. This is how I facilitate your road to ultimate ease.

The previous things you accomplished were actually Allah's gift—He empowered you, He enabled you to take that road. That's how you got to that ease. May Allah ﷻ make us all worthy of that ultimate ease.

The سَ in سَنُيَسِّرُهُۥ suggests this is ongoing—Allah ﷻ will continue to make things easier for you. Giving, taqwa, and accepting harsh truths are not easy decisions in life. They are difficult. And as you keep making these difficult, sometimes very harsh decisions, Allah ﷻ promises He will keep making things easier and easier. This theme continues throughout the surah.

Some scholars beautifully interpret this as Allah ﷻ preparing and equipping you to deal with reality. Allah ﷻ will prepare you for Paradise. This is how life prepares you for Heaven. We ask, "Who does Allah ﷻ decide goes to Heaven and who goes to Hell?" The people who will go to Heaven are those Allah ﷻ has allowed to be prepared for Heaven. How did they get prepared? By being giving people, God-conscious people, and truthful people. If they can accomplish those three things, that's Allah ﷻ preparing them for Paradise.

A profound hadith is relevant here. The Prophet ﷺ said at a funeral, "There is not one among you except that your place in Hell and your place in Paradise has already been determined. Allah ﷻ has already written it." When the Companions heard this, they asked, "If that's the case, shouldn't we just relax? The ticket is already reserved, so should we just take it easy?" 

The Prophet ﷺ responded, "No. Put yourself to work. Each one of you has been made capable for what you were designed for." I would understand this in light of the Quran: لِيَرْحَمَهُمْ (that He may have mercy on them)—the one who shows mercy, Allah ﷻ shows mercy to. Allah ﷻ made human beings to show them mercy. We were made to enter Paradise. We were made for Paradise.

In the previous surah, we learned that the default of the Hereafter is actually Paradise. That's what we're supposed to be in. So for those who deserve happiness, Allah ﷻ will facilitate their way, enable them, and empower them to do the things that people who deserve happiness do. If you do heavenly things in this world, you'll get to Heaven. That's the essence.

And for those who want to do bad things? Allah ﷻ is not closing that door either. You are just as able to do bad things. Life has given you eyes—you can look at the right thing or the wrong thing. Your choice. Hands—you can do right or wrong with them. Your choice. Feet—you can take them to good places or bad places. Your choice. He has given you that ability. The masjid is in one direction, a concert in another. You're standing right here. Two roads. You take whichever you choose. Nobody is forcing you. These are the choices you have been enabled and empowered to make.

When the Prophet ﷺ said this, he recited these very ayahs: فَأَمَّا مَنْ أَعْطَىٰ وَٱتَّقَىٰ وَصَدَّقَ بِٱلْحُسْنَىٰ فَسَنُيَسِّرُهُۥ لِلْيُسْرَىٰ—and then the opposite path.

## Ayahs 8-10

وَأَمَّا مَنۢ بَخِلَ وَٱسْتَغْنَىٰ
وَكَذَّبَ بِٱلْحُسْنَىٰ
فَسَنُيَسِّرُهُۥ لِلْعُسْرَىٰ

As for the one who was miserly and considered himself self-sufficient, and rejected the ultimate truth, then We will facilitate for him the path to ultimate difficulty.

This describes the opposite road. Remember, the efforts people make are diverse—scattered in every direction. This was one road, now here is the other. Every step has its opposite parallel.

**بَخِلَ - Was miserly**: The word بُخْل in Arabic originally means holding back something that wants to come out—like shaking a soda bottle and holding the cap on when it desperately wants to explode, or holding a water hose trying to prevent water from flowing out. That's the essence of bukhl. 

Applied to wealth, it means you have resources that naturally want to flow out to help others, but you're unnaturally holding them back. This is the exact opposite of أَعْطَىٰ (gave). The person who gives freely versus the person who withholds.

**وَٱسْتَغْنَىٰ - Considered himself self-sufficient**: The root غ‍ِنَى means to be full, to have no need. اِسْتَغْنَىٰ means to feel like you're free of need, to think you don't need to consider anything else—the bank has enough deposits, the stomach is full, no need for anything more. Another meaning of istaghna is striving to get rich, but the closer meaning here is someone who feels they have no need.

On the good path, one quality was تَقْوَىٰ—being cautious of Allah ﷻ, thinking "I need Allah's pleasure with me, I need Allah ﷻ to be happy with me, I must be careful." The opposite is someone who doesn't care, who acts however they want. 

Consider a classroom analogy: One student doesn't care whether they pass or fail. Another is extremely worried about their grade and what the teacher thinks. The way they sit in class is completely different. The concerned one sits straight, answers every question, asks "Could you repeat that, please? Should I write that down?" The other slouches, completely disconnected. That's istaghna versus taqwa.

**وَكَذَّبَ بِٱلْحُسْنَىٰ - Rejected the ultimate truth**: On the good path, it was صَدَّقَ بِٱلْحُسْنَىٰ—you accepted the most beautiful truth as it came to you. The opposite: you consider the most beautiful truth a lie. "What are you talking about? No, that's not right. I disagree with you." You refuse to accept it.

The path to goodness had three steps upward: giving, then consciousness of Allah ﷻ, then accepting truth, leading to the final destination of Paradise. Now there are three steps downward toward Hell: being cheap (already bad), not caring about anyone including Allah ﷻ (another step down), and when truth is presented to correct your ways, considering it a lie (another step down).

**فَسَنُيَسِّرُهُۥ لِلْعُسْرَىٰ - We will facilitate for him the path to ultimate difficulty**: For such a person, through these actions, We have facilitated their way to end up in the most difficult destination—Hell itself.

Some scholars note that when you accept the difficult realities of truth, struggle with them through patience, life empowers you and you become able to deal with them. But if you stick with lies, as you descend, your life only becomes harder and harder.

translation: As for he who gives and fears Allah
And believes in the best [reward]
We will ease him toward ease
But as for he who withholds and considers himself free of need
And denies the best [reward]
We will ease him toward difficulty
And what will his wealth avail him when he falls
commentary:
وَمَا يُغْنِى عَنْهُ مَالُهُۥٓ إِذَا تَرَدَّىٰٓ

And his wealth will not benefit him when he falls.

This person was cheap, holding his wealth to himself. Why? Because he felt his wealth would protect him, keep him safe and secure. He couldn't give it up. That explains his istaghna—he felt okay because he had wealth. When called to the message of truth, to giving charity, to recognizing that no matter how much you have you still depend on Allah ﷻ and wealth will not save you—he rejected it as a lie.

Allah ﷻ now calls him out on that delusion: وَمَا يُغْنِى عَنْهُ مَالُهُۥٓ—his money will not free him, will not make him as independent as he thought, إِذَا تَرَدَّىٰٓ—as he falls. 

The word تَرَدَّىٰ literally means to fall off a cliff. An animal that falls off a cliff and dies is called mutaraddi. This same imagery appears in the Quran for people falling into Hell—a deep canyon. In Surah As-Saffat, someone who made it to Paradise sees his friend in Hell and says, إِن كِدتَّ لَتُرْدِينِ (You almost threw me off the cliff too, man. I almost spent too much time with you and you almost got me).

So as he's falling, his wealth won't save him. This is the final commentary on the two roads that are vastly diverse.

translation: Indeed, [incumbent] upon Us is guidance
commentary:
إِنَّ عَلَيْنَا لَلْهُدَىٰ

Indeed, guidance is exclusively and absolutely Our responsibility.

After describing the two contrasting paths, Allah ﷻ now makes a profound statement about guidance itself. No one else can guide you—only Allah ﷻ can guide.

Guidance comprises two elements. First, information: "Where is the exit?" Someone points, "There's the exit." That's guidance. Second, accompaniment: someone holds your hand and walks you all the way to the exit. In both cases, you would say they guided you, but one is merely information while the other is active support.

When we ask Allah ﷻ for guidance, there are two steps. We ask what to do—that's information. Do this, don't do that. Say this, don't say this. Go here, don't go there. But there's more to guidance: if you accept His directions and take even one step in the right direction, He doesn't leave you by yourself. He comes with you.

Allah ﷻ is saying in this ayah: I not only give you the right information, the instructions, but if you take even a single step—remember those steps we discussed—I will absolutely be there with you, all the way to the end.

translation: And indeed, to Us belongs the Hereafter and the first [life]
commentary:
وَإِنَّ لَنَا لَلْـَٔاخِرَةَ وَٱلْأُولَىٰ

And indeed, to Us belongs the Hereafter and this world.

Notice the sequence: وَإِنَّ لَنَا لَلْـَٔاخِرَةَ وَٱلْأُولَىٰ—We alone own truly the end (the Hereafter) and the beginning (this world). Allah ﷻ didn't say "We own the beginning and the end." He said "We own the end and the beginning"—as if to say: Take this step, and I'm guaranteeing I will get you all the way to the end. And when you reach the end, you'll look back and recognize I was with you all the way from the beginning.

That's the beauty of الْـَٔاخِرَةَ وَٱلْأُولَىٰ. That's Allah's promise.

Notice what has happened in the surah's structure. It began with "Your efforts are in every direction," and now Allah ﷻ says, "I'm going to guide you." Two things are needed: our efforts and Allah's guidance. You need both. Allah ﷻ will not guide you until He sees effort from you. The effort was mentioned first, then guidance is mentioned.

This same formula was repeated earlier: When you're giving, showing taqwa, accepting truth—isn't that you making effort? And when Allah ﷻ says "I'll make your path easy for you"—isn't that Allah ﷻ guiding? Effort, then guidance. Effort, then guidance. The pattern continues throughout the surah.

translation: So I have warned you of a Fire which is blazing
commentary:
فَأَنذَرْتُكُمْ نَارًا تَلَظَّىٰ

So I have warned you of a Fire that blazes fiercely.

Now we enter the final portion of the surah. Allah ﷻ presents the warning explicitly. The new word here is تَلَظَّىٰ—when a fire is lit and its flames become intense, turning red-hot. "I'm warning you about a fire that burns intensely red."

Some asked: Where did He warn about fire earlier in the surah? He only mentioned "it won't help him as he falls." Now Allah ﷻ fills in that image, saying: By the way, what you're falling into is a Fire that will burn with extreme intensity.

Others suggest this refers to a special place within Hell—not just Hell generally, but an especially scorching section. The ayah can be understood as: I'm warning you about a Fire that couldn't possibly be any hotter.

translation: None will [enter to] burn therein except the most wretched one
commentary:
لَا يَصْلَىٰهَآ إِلَّا ٱلْأَشْقَى

None will enter it except the most wretched.

This ayah must be carefully understood through contrast. When a driving manual says "If you cross a red light, you'll get a ticket," that means there may be other rules too—you could also get a ticket for speeding, driving on the wrong side, etc. Many ways to get a ticket exist.

But if the manual said "Nobody will get a ticket except people who cross red lights," that would mean it's the only violation. You could drive on the wrong side of the highway without consequence.

Similarly, Allah ﷻ didn't say "The most wretched will enter Hell." He said لَا يَصْلَىٰهَآ إِلَّا ٱلْأَشْقَى—nobody will enter this Fire except the most wretched. In other words, other than the most wretched, they won't be entering this particular Fire.

This is why some scholars said there must be other people in Hell too, but this is a special section for the most wretched, which nobody will enter except them.

Now, who are these most wretched people? It's one thing to hear truth and reject it. But it's completely different when the Messenger of Allah ﷺ is standing before you, speaking truth to your face, and you reject him. When you are cheap despite having the living embodiment of generosity before you. When you have the living example of taqwa before you and you still show istaghna. When you have the living example of tasdiq bil-husna and you practice takdhib bil-husna.

Nobody could be more miserable than a disbeliever—and the most miserable of all disbelievers is the one who was in the company of the Prophet ﷺ and then rejected him. These are the most wretched of people. The previous surah highlighted the worst of that society with تَبَّتْ يَدَا أَبِي لَهَبٍ (May the hands of Abu Lahab be ruined). Now this surah says: Even in this society, the worst of the worst—nobody deserves that level of Hell except these people.

Remember the sequencing earlier: You saw one level of bad (being cheap), then worse (not caring about anyone, not even Allah ﷻ), then rejecting truth (absolutely the worst). Bad, worse, absolutely the worst. And what's the absolute worst? الْأَشْقَى—the most miserable. None but they will be thrown inside and burned by it.

translation: Who had denied and turned away
commentary:
ٱلَّذِى كَذَّبَ وَتَوَلَّىٰ

The one who rejected the truth and turned away.

What's his description? كَذَّبَ—the one who called it all a lie. The word كَذَّبَ appears twice now, reinforcing and intensifying the meaning. The one who rejected the truth, who dismissed it, وَتَوَلَّىٰ—and turned away arrogantly. I would add "arrogantly"—he simply turned his back, didn't care.

This is his crime. Notice that تَوَلَّىٰ is an echo of اِسْتَغْنَىٰ—to not care, to think you don't need something. Obviously you walk away from what you don't need. So it's two sides of the same coin.

translation: But the righteous one will avoid it
commentary:
وَسَيُجَنَّبُهَا ٱلْأَتْقَى

And the most righteous will be kept far away from it.

Beautiful language: يُجَنَّبُ means to keep something far, far away from something. The Hell that Allah ﷻ described—blazing with heat that couldn't be any more intense—there's a group of people who will be kept far, far, far away from it.

Who are they? الْأَتْقَى—the best of the best, the people of the most taqwa, the most conscious of Allah ﷻ.

This ayah might worry us. The ayah says unless you have the most taqwa, you won't be kept that far from the Fire. But remember what I explained earlier about "nobody will get a ticket except...": Allah ﷻ didn't say here لَن يُجَنَّبُهَا إِلَّا ٱلْأَتْقَى (None will be kept away from it except the most righteous), parallel to لَا يَصْلَىٰهَآ إِلَّا ٱلْأَشْقَى. 

If He had said that, it would mean the only group kept away from that Fire would be the most righteous. But He said the most righteous will be kept away from it—leaving open the possibility that others who are simply righteous, or among the siddiqin, or the shuhada, or the salihin—maybe they're not al-atqa, but they're on a good path—might also be kept away.

Allah ﷻ is highlighting by contrast: the worst will be here, the best will be here. But when He gets to the best, He doesn't say "only the best," because if He meant only the best, He would have used the construction لَن يُجَنَّبُهَا إِلَّا in the same form as لَا يَصْلَىٰهَآ إِلَّا. 

SubhanAllah, that's Allah's mercy in this ayah—He didn't close that door on the rest of us. We may have taqwa, perhaps even one atom's worth of taqwa, but we may not be al-atqa. Yet hope remains.

وَسَيُجَنَّبُهَا ٱلْأَتْقَى—the one with the most conscience of Allah ﷻ will be kept at a far distance from it. Who is this person of the most consciousness of Allah ﷻ? What makes them al-atqa? Okay, I'm not there—but how do you get there? What makes someone this amazing?

Allah ﷻ describes it.

translation: [He] who gives [from] his wealth to purify himself
commentary:
ٱلَّذِى يُؤْتِى مَالَهُۥ يَتَزَكَّىٰ

The one who gives his wealth to purify himself.

الَّذِى يُؤْتِى مَالَهُۥ يَتَزَكَّىٰ—the one who gives his wealth in order to nourish himself, to grow himself, to purify himself. When he gives charity, he's not even thinking primarily about the recipient. He's thinking: This is going to benefit me. How? It will make me purer before Allah ﷻ. It's removing my sins. It's washing away my evil deeds. It's making me wealthier spiritually and better prepared to meet Allah ﷻ, because Allah ﷻ accepts only that which is good and pure. Giving sadaqah is like giving away your own filth, your own sins.

So he gives money in order to purify himself. This is why people say this describes Abu Ad-Darda (RA), who gave simply because he wanted one tree in Paradise. "That's all. Good enough for me. Good enough."

Notice: you would think al-atqa, the one most conscious of Allah ﷻ, would be described primarily in terms of worship, right? But the description emphasizes giving—giving to people—while the motivation is to be better ready to meet Allah ﷻ. The greatest sign of your taqwa is how much you give. The people of the most taqwa are the people of the most giving.

translation: And not [giving] for anyone who has [done him] a favor to be rewarded
commentary:
وَمَا لِأَحَدٍ عِندَهُۥ مِن نِّعْمَةٍ تُجْزَىٰٓ

And not for anyone who has done him a favor expecting compensation.

وَمَا لِأَحَدٍ عِندَهُۥ مِن نِّعْمَةٍ تُجْزَىٰٓ—when he gives, there's not a single person he has done a favor to that needs repaying. In other words, no strings attached. He didn't give because "I owe him from before" or "this is tit for tat" or there's some other agenda. He's not expecting "I'm giving now, but later he'd better remember I did this for him."

Remember when you freed a slave in that society, they traditionally owed you a favor. But when Abu Bakr (RA) used to free slaves, he didn't expect favors. "You're free to go. I'm not going to ask anything of you anymore." No strings attached when he gives.

وَمَا لِأَحَدٍ عِندَهُۥ مِن نِّعْمَةٍ تُجْزَىٰٓ—he doesn't have a favor that should be compensated. It's not a payoff for something done in the past. He's giving only for his own benefit, only to purify himself.

translation: But only seeking the countenance of his Lord, Most High
commentary:
إِلَّا ٱبْتِغَآءَ وَجْهِ رَبِّهِ ٱلْأَعْلَىٰ

Except seeking the Face of his Lord, the Most High.

Now Allah ﷻ adds another qualifier: إِلَّا ٱبْتِغَآءَ وَجْهِ رَبِّهِ ٱلْأَعْلَىٰ—the only pursuit in his mind, the only thing he wants to see when he gives charity, is that he's in pursuit of the Face of his Most High Master.

Here you have the picture of a free man—Abu Bakr (RA)—freeing a slave, while in his mind he himself is the slave begging to meet his Master. That's the beauty of this.

He just wants to see the Face of Allah ﷻ. Some people on that Day—يَوْمَئِذٍ نَّاضِرَةٌ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهَا نَاظِرَةٌ (Some faces that Day will be radiant, looking at their Lord)—their faces are brilliantly lit because they're staring at their Lord. Allah's light illuminates their faces. وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ مُّسْفِرَةٌ ضَاحِكَةٌ مُّسْتَبْشِرَةٌ—faces filled with light. Why? Because they're staring straight at Allah ﷻ and He's looking back at them.

إِلَّا ٱبْتِغَآءَ وَجْهِ رَبِّهِ ٱلْأَعْلَىٰ—all they wanted was to be able to see the Face of their Lord.

By the way, seeing the face has another meaning. When you disappoint somebody, you avoid eye contact. When you let someone down, you don't look them in the eye. Your eyes are humbled. The Quran says: خَاشِعَةً أَبْصَارُهُمْ تَرْهَقُهُمْ ذِلَّةٌ (Their eyes humbled, humiliation covering them). But when you've made somebody proud, when you've earned their pleasure, you want to look at them. You want to see how proud they are of you, how happy they are with you.

That's ابْتِغَاءَ وَجْهِ رَبِّهِ—seeking the Face of the Lord.

translation: And he is going to be satisfied
commentary:
وَلَسَوْفَ يَرْضَىٰ

And he will surely be pleased.

وَلَسَوْفَ يَرْضَىٰ—and very soon he's going to be content. He's going to be pleased.

Allah ﷻ is describing people who are never content in this life. They keep wanting to do more. They never feel "I've done enough to meet Allah ﷻ. I've done enough to be ready." But very soon—when Allah ﷻ takes them all the way to that ease, when their struggle in this life is done and they finally meet Allah ﷻ—He's guaranteeing them: you're going to be happy.

وَلَسَوْفَ يَرْضَىٰ—may Allah ﷻ guarantee us that, and may He give us that pleasure of meeting Him and overlook our shortcomings.
